content.full_text:
Copy also filed:
18702

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

March 30, 1981

Jesse A. Combs
Lake County Board of
Supervisors
255 N. Forbes St.
Lakeport, CA 95453

A-81-03-048

Dear Mr. Combs:

This is in response to your request of February 6, 1981, for advice concerning a possible conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act. With your request, you enclosed a letter to you from Robert L. Bridges, Senior Deputy County Counsel for Lake County. In that letter, Mr. Bridges outlined in detail your conflict of interest problem based on the following facts.

(1) You have an investment interest in United Land & Timber, Inc. (ULT) of greater than $10,000 but less than $100,000. You own all of the stock in that Company. You are the President, as well as the General Manager, of ULT. Over the last couple of years, ULT's gross income has ranged from $350,000 to $700,000.

(2) ULT owns heavy equipment used in earth moving.

(3) ULT has an informal sharing and work arrangement with Challenge Construction. Specifically, ULT shares yard and office space with Challenge Construction and it has agreed to "wet" rent ULT's equipment for use on all geothermal projects it secures. You have agreed to act as an advisor on these jobs for free.

(4) ULT will receive rent when its equipment is used.

(5) Geothermal projects are sometimes put to bid, sometimes negotiated. When put out to bid, usually about six company's submit bids. When negotiated, one company is singled out by the geothermal company and a price is worked out.

Jesse A. Combs
March 30, 1981
Page Two


     (6) Geothermal projects will bring a gross of
between $75,000 and $250,000 to a successful bidder or
negotiator.

     The questions you posed were whether there is a violation
of the Political Reform Act if ULT does work on geothermal
projects for which:

     1. Permits were issued before you assumed office
as county supervisor.

     2. Permits are issued during your tenure of office.

     It is important to realize that the Political Reform
Act does not regulate contracts. The focus of the Act is
rather to prohibit public officials from using their positions
to influence a governmental decision. Therefore, there is
nothing in the Act which would prohibit ULT from working on
any geothermal projects, nor would there be a conflict of
interest with respect to decisions made before you took
office.

     The conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act are contained in Government Code Sections 87100-
87103,1/ which provide in relevant part that:

     No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
to know he has a financial interest.

                                        Section 87100.

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

     (a) Any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000).

                          . . .

_____________________________
1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise stated.

Jesse A. Combs
March 30, 1981
Page Three


     (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard
to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior
to the time when the decision is made.

     (d) Any business entity in which the public official
is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management.

                         . . .

                    Section 87103.

     You have a financial interest in ULT within the meaning
of Section 87103 because:

     1. ULT is a business entity in which you have an
        investment greater than $1,000.

     2. ULT has been a source of income to you of $250 or
        more within the past year.

     3. ULT is a business entity in which you are an officer
        and hold a position of management.

     Thus, you may not make or participate in making any
decisions which would have a foreseeable material financial
effect on ULT.

     You may also have a financial interest in Challenge.
Section 82030 defines "income" in relevant part as including

     a pro rata share of any income of any business
entity ... in which the individual ... owns ... a 10
percent interest or greater...

     Since you are the sole stockholder in ULT, your pro rata
share of income to ULT is 100 percent. Therefore, if Challenge
has been a source of income to ULT of $250 or more within
the last year, then Challenge is also a source of income to
you. If Challenge is a source of income to you, then you
cannot make or participate in making any decisions which
would have a foreseeable material financial effect on Challenge,
whether or not those decisions impact on ULT.

Jesse A. Combs
March 30, 1981
Page Four


     Even if Challenge is not a source of income to you, you
should refrain from making or participating in decisions
respecting Challenge which will foreseeably benefit ULT.
Specifically, you should refrain from participating in decisions
on geothermal projects in which Challenge is a forseeable
bidder.

     The Thorner opinion, 1 FPPC Opinion 198 (75-089 Dec. 4,
1975), addressed the issue of foreseeability under facts
similar to those presented here. Thorner considered the
example of a director of a municipal water district who
asked if he could participate in a decision on a water project.
A contractor, who in the past had purchased only from the
director's business, was preparing to bid on this project.
The Commission found that if the contractor's bid was likely
to be successful, there was then a foreseeable financial
effect on the director's business.

     These facts are analogous to your own circumstances.
Challenge and ULT have an informal agreement that only ULT
equipment will be leased for geothermal projects in which
Challenge is the successful bidder. Thus, it is foreseeable
that a decision respecting a geothermal project in which it
is likely that Challenge will be a successful bidder, would
have a financial effect on ULT.

     This leaves only the issue of materiality of the financial
effect. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 sets forth the guidelines
for determining whether a foreseeable financial effect is
also material. That regulation provides in relevant part
that:

          (a) The financial effect of a governmental decision
     on a financial interest of a public official is material
     if the decision will have a significant effect on the
     business entity, real property or source of income in
     question.

          (b) In determining whether it is reasonably fore-
     seeable that the effects of a governmental decision
     will be significant within the meaning of the general
     standard set forth in paragraph (a), consideration
     should be given to the following factors:

               (1) Whether, in the case of a business entity
          in which the public official holds a direct or
          indirect investment of one thousand dollars ($1,000)

Jesse A. Combs
March 30, 1981
Page Five


                or more in the case of a business entity in which
                the public official is a director, officer, partner,
                employee, trustee or holds any position of management,
                the effect of the decision will be to increase or
                decrease:

                    (A)  The annualized gross revenues by
                the lesser of:

                    1.  One hundred thousand dollars
                ($100,000); or

                    2.  One percent if it is one thousand
                dollars ($1,000) or more; or

                (B)  Annual net income by the lesser of:

                    1.  Fifty thousand dollars ($50,000); or

                    2.  One half of one percent if it
                is one thousand dollars ($1,000) or
                more; or

                (C)  Current assets or liabilities by
                the lesser of:

                    1.  One hundred thousand dollars
                ($100,000); or

                    2.  One half of one percent if it
                is one thousand dollars ($1,000) or
                more.

                    Current assets are deemed to be
                decreased by the amount of any expenses
                incurred as a result of a governmental
                decision.

                    . . .

                (3)  Whether, in the case of a source of
                income as defined in Government Code Section 87103(c),
                of two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more received
                by or promised to a public official within 12
                months prior to the time the decision is made:

Jesse A. Combs
March 30, 1981
Page Six


                    (A) The effect of the decision will be
          to directly increase or decrease the amount
          of income (other than rents) to be received
          by the official by one hundred dollars ($100)
          or more; or

                    (B) There is a nexus between the govern-
          mental decision and the purpose for which the
          official receives income; or

                    (C) In the case of a source of income
          which is a business entity, the business
          entity will be affected in a manner described
          in subsection (b)(1) above; or

                              . . .

     Under this regulation there are several tests for materi-
ality. Applying them to the facts at hand, we find that the
financial effect of a decision is material under the following
circumstances:

     1. If the annualized gross revenues to ULT will be
$5,250-2/ or more.

     2. If ULT is a source of income to you and if the
effect of the decision will be to increase or decrease that
income by $100 or more.

     3. If Challenge is a source of income to you and the
effect of the decision on Challenge will be to increase or
decrease its annualized gross revenues, annual net income or
current assets and liabilities in the manner outlined in
Section 1870 2(b)(1).

     In conclusion, you should refrain from making or partici-
pating in any decision which will have a foreseeable material
financial effect on ULT. If Challenge is a source of income
to you, you must also refrain from making or participating
in decisions which will have a foreseeable material financial
effect on Challenge, irrespective of their impact on ULT.


_____________________________

     2/ This figure is 1 percent of the average of the
gross revenues of the last two years.

Jesse A. Combs
March 30, 1981
Page Seven


     Please let me know if you have any further questions on
this matter.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]

                                        Stella Connell Levy
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

SCL:kp

COUNTY OF LAKE
BOARD OF SUPERVISORS

Courthouse — 255 N. Forbes Street
Lakeport, California 95453
Telephone 707/263-2367

HOWARD D. DAY
District 1

District 2

WALTER C. WILCOX
District 3

RAYMOND L. COUGH
District 4

JESSE A. COMBS
District 5

February 6, 1981

Ms. Sarah Cameron
Deputy Chief for Conflicts of Interest
Legal Division
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, Ca. 95004

Dear Ms. Cameron:

Pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18730, I request an opinion on No.
83114- Requests For and Issuances of Opinions; Advice. Thank you.

Sincerely,

Jesse A. Combs
Supervisor District 5

JAC/ces

COUNTY OF LAKE
Office of the County Counsel

FEB 10 1981 Courthouse -- 255 N. Forbes Street
LAKEPORT, CALIFORNIA 95453
Telephone 707-263-2321

February 5, 1981                                    81-3

SUPERVISORS JESS COMBS
Board of Supervisors
Courthouse--255 N. Forbes St.
Lakeport, California  95453

Re: Conflict of Interest Question

Honorable Jess Combs,

You have requested the opinion of this office concerning a
conflict of interest question. Specifically, you desire to
know if there is any conflict of interest problem, if your
company's equipment does work on geothermal projects for which:

(1) Permits were issued before you assumed office.

(2) Projects which are permitted during your tenure of
office.

The facts are understood to be as follows:

(1) You have an investment interest in United Land &
Timber, Inc. (ULT) of greater than $10,000 but less than $100,000.
You own all of the stock in that Company. You are the President,
as well as the General Manager, of ULT. Over the last couple
of years, the ULT's gross income has ranged from $350,000 to
$700,000.

(2) ULT owns heavy equipment used in earth moving.

(3) ULT has an informal sharing and work arrangement with
Challenge Construction. Specifically, ULT shares yard and
office space with Challenge Construction and it has agreed to
"wet" rent your equipment for use on all geothermal projects it
secures. You have agreed to act as an advisor on these jobs
for free.

(4) ULT will receive rent when its equipment is used.

(5) Geothermal projects are sometimes put to bid and
sometimes negotiated. When put out to bid, usually about six
Company's will submit bids. When negotiated, one company is
singled out by the geothermal company and a price is worked
out.

(6) Geothermal projects will bring a gross of between
$75,000.00 and $250,000 to a successful bidder or negotiator.

SENIOR DEPUTIES
Robert L. Bridges
Michael D. Ott

DEPUTIES
David T. Hayden
DeVon L. Workman

SCHOOLS
W. Wood Merrill
1152 South Main Street
Lakeport, California 95453
707/263-3080

At this point, it should be noted that the laws concerning
conflict of interest have no direct effect on your private
activity. In your private capacity, you can work or go with
anyone you like. You can contract with anyone (excepting the
County) you like. The state conflict of interest laws do not
restrict private individuals in their private activities. What
they do is to restrict a public official's ability to act or
participate in an official capacity on matters which may affect
their own pocketbook. However, as the analysis will show,
your decision to vote or participate on a given matter can have
the indirect effect of limiting your private activity. With
this basic information, the specific legal analysis will begin.

The law which is pertinent to the present question is the
Political Reform Act of 1974*, Government Code §§ 81000 et
seq. Section 87100 provides,

"No public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in making
or in any way attempt to use his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest."

The practical effect of this law is that when a public
official has a financial interest in a given matter, he must
refrain from voting, participating or in any way attempting to
use his official position to influence a governmental decision.
A simple and obvious example of this principle in action would
be the McCulloch-Combs Geothermal Use Permit. Assuming you
were the owner of that property and that you were on the board
(obviously you weren't) when that matter came up for hearing
before the board of supervisors, the law would require you to
abstain from voting or participating on that matter in your
official capacity. You would still have been free to participate
as a member of the public. The basic issue you must keep in
mind is if you have a financial interest you must avoid any
form of official action to affect your financial interest.

Section 87300 provides in pertinent part,

"An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally, on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth more than
one thousand dollars ($1,000);

* All further statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

- 2 -

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth more
than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

(c) Any source of income, other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course
of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value received by or promised to
the public official within twelve months prior to the
time when the decision is made; or

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management..."
(Emphasis added)

Under Sections 87100 and 87300, several elements must be
present before a public official is required to disqualify
himself from participation in a governmental decision. First,
it must be reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision
will have a financial effect. Second, the anticipated financial
effect must be on a financial interest of the official, as
defined in Sections 87103(a) through (d). Third, the anticipated
financial effect must be material. And fourth, the governmental
decision's anticipated financial effect on the official's financial
interest must be distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally.

There can be no doubt but that you have a financial interest in
ULT with the meaning of Section 87103, since it is a business entity
in which you have a direct investment worth more than $1,000. Nor
can there be any serious doubt that if ULT supplied equipment to any
geothermal project which is made possible because of a permit approval,
the effect of the permit approval on ULT will be distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally. Although there may be
several possible suppliers of equipment to a geothermal project,
they cannot be considered to constitute a large enough group to
qualify as the "public generally", or even a significant segment of
the public generally. In determining the materiality question,
Title 2 of the California Administrative Code sets forth guidelines
by which to judge this issue. Section 18702 states in pertinent
part,

"(a) The financial effect of a governmental
decision on a financial interest of a public official
is material if the decision will have a significant
effect on the business entity, real property or
source of income in question.

- 3 -

(b)  In determining whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the effects of a governmental deci-
sion will be significant within the meaning of the
general standard set forth in paragraph (a), consider-
ation should be given to the following factors:

     (1)  Whether, in the case of a business
entity in which the public official holds a direct or
indirect investment of one thousand dollars ($1,000)
or more or in the case of a business entity in which
the public official is a director, officer, partner,
employee, trustee or holds any position of management,
the effect of the decision will be to increase or
decrease

          (A)  The annualized gross revenues by
the lessee of:

               1.   One hundred thousand dollars
($100,000); or

               2.   One percent if it is one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more;..."

Since ULT has had a gross annual income of between $350,000 and
$700,000 and a geothermal project will bring a gross income of
between $75,000 and $250,000, it is clear that the project will
increase the gross revenues by more than one percent or $100,000.
Accordingly, it is quite clear that a geothermal decision which brought ULT work would meet the materiality test.
Therefore, the only element in question with respect to you on
geothermal decisions is foreseeability.  In any instance in which
this element is present to the extent required by the Political
Reform Act you will be legally required to disqualify yourself from
any geothermal decision.

The Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC) has issued its
Opinion No. 75-089 on a very similar set of facts.  This opinion
concerned a water district director (McPhail) who owned and operated
a ready mix concrete operation in the water district.  The question
was whether McPhail could vote on variances from a water connection
moratorium.  Among others, the Commission discussed the following
two fact situations:

     "(c)  A contractor, who is a regular customer
of McPhail's and who normally buys principally or
only from McPhail's, is preparing to bid or has bid
on the project and, if awarded the contract, probably
will purchase some of McPhail's products for the job;

     (d)  The type of contractor described in (c)
already has been awarded the contract but has not
yet purchased or agreed to purchase any of McPhail's
products for the project;"


- 4 -

Starting on page 9 of the FTC opinion it states,

"There is a significant difference between the
two situations [cfd]. In example (d), although
there is no certainty that McPhail's will receive
business, there is a high probability that it will
since the contractor who has been awarded the contract
is a regular customer. Although there is no agreement,
express or implied, cf. People v. Deysher, 2 Cal. 2d
141 (1934), between McPhail's and the contractor,
there is, without question, a sufficient likelihood
that McPhail's will receive business to make the
financial effect on Director McPhail "reasonably
foreseeable." (Emphasis added)

"In example (c), on the other hand, an extra
degree of remoteness is added to the foreseeability
of the financial effect by reason of the fact that
the contractor has not yet been awarded the contract,
but merely has entered a bid or is preparing to do
so. McPhail's will benefit only if the contractor's
bid is successful and the contractor follows its
normal practice of purchasing from McPhail's."

"In the case of a contractor who in the past has
purchased only from McPhail's, Inc., it is reasonably
foreseeable that Director McPhail's decision could
have a material financial effect upon McPhail's, Inc.
However, where the contractor-applicant purchases
from vendors other than McPhail's, Inc. we cannot
conclude that a financial effect on McPhail's is
reasonably foreseeable. Nevertheless, if in this
latter example, there are facts indicating that the
contractor's bid is likely to be successful, the
financial effect would be reasonably foreseeable
since the situation then becomes analogous to example
(d)." (Emphasis added)

It can readily be seen that your relationship with Challenge
Construction is just like the relationship of McPhail to a contractor
who only purchased from McPhail. If Challenge secures a geothermal
job by bid or negotiation, you will definitely receive the work.
Accordingly, this office must conclude that if:

(1) Challenge has entered a bid or is preparing to do so; or,

(2) Challenge has already been awarded or bid or has negotiated
or is negotiating a bid.

- 5 -

It will be reasonably foreseeable that your decision to
approve a geothermal project would have a material financial
effect on ULT. Therefore, you will be faced with the following
choices:

     1.  Participate and or vote on a geothermal project and
then have ULT refrain from renting to Challenge on the project;
or,

     2.  Abstain from participation or voting on the geothermal
project and then proceed to have ULT work on the project.

                              CONCLUSION

     Based on the foregoing, it is apparent that your financial
interests are too close to a geothermal project for you to
proceed to freely and fully act on projects which you have
approved in your official capacity and then work on them too.
For projects which were approved before, you come into office,
you may proceed as you wish.  However, should you choose to do
so, you should be aware that any disputes which arise concerning
county inspectors and your equipment operators would require
you to refrain from any attempt to use your official position
to influence, make, or participate in the resolution of the
dispute.

     If you are not satisfied with this opinion and desire further
legal advice, the law provides an avenue for you to request an
opinion from the FPPC.  See Gov. Code § 83114.  A complete copy
of this law is attached for your convenience.  This route could
give you both civil and criminal protection.

     If you have further questions on this matter, please feel
free to contact this office.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        CHARLES D. HAUGHTON
                                        County Counsel

                                        [signature]

                                        ROBERT L. BRIDGES
                                        Senior Deputy

RLB:ps

sections.conclusion:
Based on the foregoing, it is apparent that your financial interests are too close to a geothermal project for you to proceed to freely and fully act on projects which you have approved in your official capacity and then work on them too. For projects which were approved before, you come into office, you may proceed as you wish. However, should you choose to do so, you should be aware that any disputes which arise concerning county inspectors and your equipment operators would require you to refrain from any attempt to use your official position to influence, make, or participate in the resolution of the dispute.

If you are not satisfied with this opinion and desire further legal advice, the law provides an avenue for you to request an opinion from the FPPC. See Gov. Code § 83114. A complete copy of this law is attached for your convenience. This route could give you both civil and criminal protection.

CONCLUSION: Based on the foregoing, it is apparent that your financial interests are too close to a geothermal project for you to proceed to freely and fully act on projects which you have approved in your official capacity and then work on them too. For projects which were approved before, you come into office, you may proceed as you wish. However, should you choose to do so, you should be aware that any disputes which arise concerning county inspectors and your equipment operators would require you to refrain from any attempt to use your official position to influence, make, or participate in the resolution of the dispute.

If you are not satisfied with this opinion and desire further legal advice, the law provides an avenue for you to request an opinion from the FPPC. See Gov. Code § 83114. A complete copy of this law is attached for your convenience. This route could give you both civil and criminal protection.
